result.root_cause: The site's OTP cells are input[type=number] elements only ~25px wide, styled with CSS appearance:none to hide the native number spin-button. The divergence is a pure form-control rendering difference in how the two engines treat appearance:none on input[type=number]. Chrome (Blink) treats appearance:none as suppressing the native number spin-button, so it is not laid out and the tiny cell keeps its content box (clientWidth about 23 of the 25px offsetWidth) and the typed or placeholder digit renders. Firefox (Gecko) does NOT remove the input[type=number] spin-button for appearance:none; in Gecko only appearance:textfield or -moz-appearance:textfield removes the spinner. So the anonymous number spin-box is laid out inside the 25px cell and consumes essentially the entire content box (clientWidth about 0 to 1), leaving no room for the digit, so each cell renders only the up and down spinner chevrons (the drop-downs in the report) and the entered number is not displayed. No JavaScript error, network failure, or user-agent sniffing is involved; the DOM and computed styles are identical across browsers. The site relied on Chrome and WebKit behaviour where appearance:none alone strips the number spinner, which is not how Gecko handles type=number. Using appearance:textfield, or a type=text field with inputmode=numeric, would render consistently in both.
result.firefox_findings: On the real jobs.abbott apply page, entering an email and clicking Get OTP injects six input[type=number] cells with class otp-field-input (autocomplete=one-time-code, placeholder shown as underscores), each with computed appearance:none and offsetWidth 25px. Every cell renders the native Firefox number spin-button: offsetWidth is 25 but clientWidth is 0 for every cell, because the anonymous spin-box takes the whole content area, so a typed digit has no room and is not shown; the cells look like up and down drop-downs. A Request limit reached message only appears on repeated Get OTP clicks (a server-side rate limiter, unrelated to the rendering bug). The reduced test case at /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-ldqgs9kd/2053077-default.html confirms it in isolation: a 25px input[type=number] with appearance:none and value 8 reports clientWidth of 1 and the screenshot shows only spinner chevrons with no visible 8; the same-width input[type=number] with appearance:textfield reports clientWidth of 19 and shows 8; an input[type=text] reports clientWidth of 19 and shows 8. So the digit disappears specifically for type=number combined with appearance:none.
result.chrome_findings: Chrome, running the identical steps on the real site, reveals the same six cells with identical markup and computed style: input[type=number] with class otp-field-input, appearance:none, offsetWidth 25px. Chrome removes the number spin-button for appearance:none, so clientWidth is 23 for each cell (content box preserved) and the typed digit is centered and visible, clean square boxes with no spinner. In the reduced test case, Chrome reports clientWidth of 23 for all three rows (appearance:none type=number, appearance:textfield type=number, and type=text), so the spin-button is removed in every number case and the digit always renders.
result.difference: Identical DOM and identical computed appearance:none in both browsers; the only difference is engine treatment of appearance:none on input[type=number]. Chrome removes the spin-button so the content box clientWidth is about 23 of 25px and the digit is visible (works as expected). Firefox retains the spin-button so the content box clientWidth is about 0 to 1 and the cell shows only up and down spinner arrows (drop-down) and the entered number is not displayed (the reported bug). The reduced test case isolates the trigger: switching only appearance:none to appearance:textfield on the same 25px number input, or switching type=number to type=text, restores clientWidth of about 19 and shows the digit in Firefox, proving appearance:none combined with type=number is the exact condition.
result.confidence: high. The reduced test case reproduces the divergence deterministically (Firefox clientWidth 1 vs Chrome 23), a screenshot shows the spinner-only rendering, and control rows (appearance:textfield and type=text) confirm the exact trigger with no JS or network involvement.
result.steps: 1. Read the verified reproduction script at /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-ldqgs9kd/2053077-default.repro.mjs, which drives the real page (URL from the report: the jobs.abbott us/en/apply page with jobSeqNo ABLAUS31152679ENUSEXTERNAL and step 1 stepname applicantAcknowledgment) in both browsers: it fills the email field, clicks button.otp-send-btn, waits for input.otp-field-input, and measures per-cell type, computed appearance, offsetWidth and clientWidth, recording Firefox clientWidth 0 vs Chrome clientWidth 23. 2. From that evidence, formed the hypothesis that the divergence is appearance:none on input[type=number]: Chrome strips the number spin-button, Firefox does not (only appearance:textfield or -moz-appearance:textfield does in Gecko). 3. Wrote a minimal reduced HTML test case to /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-ldqgs9kd/2053077-default.html with three rows of 25px inputs: Row A is input[type=number] with appearance:none (mirrors the site), Row B is input[type=number] with appearance:textfield (control), Row C is input[type=text] (control); a page script exposes a probe function returning each input's type, computed appearance, offsetWidth and clientWidth. 4. Loaded the file URL for that test case in Firefox and Chrome via the DevTools navigate_page tools. 5. Ran the probe via evaluate_script in both. Firefox: Row A clientWidth 1, Row B 19, Row C 19. Chrome: all rows clientWidth 23, confirming the digit-hiding condition is exactly appearance:none combined with type=number, present only in Firefox. 6. Captured a Firefox screenshot showing Row A with only up and down spinner chevrons and no 8, while Rows B and C show 8 clearly, matching the report. 7. Concluded the root cause is the browser difference in handling appearance:none on input[type=number] and submitted this diagnosis (analysis only, no fix).

result.root_cause: Browser interop difference in how the CSS :target pseudo-class is recomputed after the referenced element is removed and re-created with the same id. The site reveals the login popup via .modal hidden and .modal:target shown. The document opens at the login fragment and a matching id-login element already exists in the delivered markup, so during navigation each engine binds the indicated element to that node. A Vue app then mounts and re-renders, discarding that node and building a replacement with the same id. Chrome updates its indicated-element pointer to the replacement so the reveal rule applies and the popup shows; Firefox does not update after the original detaches, so the replacement never counts as the indicated element and the popup stays hidden. This is pointer recomputation, not markup, script errors, or blocked requests.
result.firefox_findings: In Firefox the popup is absent. querySelector for the login id returns the element with innerHTML length 2557 and location.hash equal to the login fragment, but getComputedStyle reports visibility hidden and opacity zero and matches with the :target selector returns false, so the reveal rule stays inactive. Re-navigating the fragment afterward makes the match become true and reveals the popup, showing the indicated element was not recomputed after the node was rebuilt. The console shows the same undefined-url ReferenceError seen in Chrome, unrelated to the popup. Reported build is version 153.
result.chrome_findings: In Chrome the popup renders immediately. querySelector returns the same element with innerHTML length 2557 and location.hash equal to the login fragment, but getComputedStyle reports visibility visible and opacity one and matches with the :target selector returns true, so the reveal rule is active even though the framework rebuilt the node after load. Logs include an undefined-url ReferenceError plus a Network Error and one ERR_HTTP2_PROTOCOL_ERROR subresource, none affecting the popup. The only contrast with Firefox is that Chrome rebinds the indicated element to the rebuilt node.
result.difference: All measurable state is equal across engines except whether the current login element satisfies the :target selector. Chrome true so the reveal rule fires and the popup shows; Firefox false so it never fires and the popup is absent. The trigger is the framework discarding the node navigation latched onto and substituting a same-id node; Chrome relatches, Firefox does not. The reduced page shows the same result: visible in Chrome, hidden in Firefox.
result.confidence: high. The reduced page isolates one variable, swapping the fragment-referenced node at DOMContentLoaded, and yields the same Chrome-shows Firefox-hides outcome; forcing re-navigation in Firefox restores the popup.
result.steps: 1. Read the repro script. 2. Tested a late-append page: both engines hid it. 3. Opened the real page in each engine: Chrome shows, Firefox hides. 4. Fetched the document in Chrome and found a modal-class login element already in the markup at byte 11926 plus framework markers. 5. Swept timings locally: rebuild during parse showed in both; rebuild after load in neither; rebuild at DOMContentLoaded only in Chrome. 6. Authored the reduced page 2040305-default.html swapping the node at DOMContentLoaded. 7. Loaded it: Chrome visible with true match, Firefox hidden with false match. 8. Confirmed logged errors appear in Chrome too.

result.root_cause: Pure CSS layout-engine difference in how Firefox vs Chrome resolve the used inline (width) size of an auto-width, block-level replaced
result.firefox_findings: On https://drmeth.com/ at viewport 1366x768 (Firefox 153.0), document.querySelector('iframe') (class aspect-video max-h-full max-w-full, src /game/index.html, no width/height attribute, no inline style) has getBoundingClientRect 300x169 and getComputedStyle width:300px, height:168.75px, aspect-ratio:16/9, max-width:100%, max-height:100%, display:block, flex:0 1 auto, align-self:auto, min-width:auto. Its parent flex container measures 1366px wide, so the iframe occupies only ~22% of available width and is centered — the tiny box with overlapping/unscaled game UI from the report. list_console_messages (level=error) returned none, and no failed/blocked network request explains the sizing. Firefox resolves the auto-width iframe to the CSS default intrinsic replaced width of 300px.
result.chrome_findings: Running the identical steps in Chrome (HeadlessChrome 151), the same iframe (same classes, no width/height attribute, no inline style) is sized to fill its container: getBoundingClientRect width equals the parent's inline size (1905px with the actual window width; computed cssWidth 1905px, height 1071.56px via 16/9), so the game renders full-size and correctly laid out. All other getComputedStyle values match Firefox (aspect-ratio:16/9, max-width/height:100%, display:block, flex:0 1 auto). No console errors and no network differences. Chrome stretches the width-less block-level replaced iframe to the available container width rather than falling back to the 300px intrinsic default.
result.difference: Same DOM, same CSS, same network/console (no errors in either). The only observable divergence is the iframe's resolved width: Firefox = 300px (its default intrinsic replaced width) so the game collapses to ~300x169 and overlaps; Chrome = full container width (e.g. 1905px, clamped by 16/9 and max-height) so the game is full-size. The reduced test isolates it: with aspect-ratio:16/9; max-width/height:100% and no width, inside a display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center 400px container — an
result.confidence: high — the reduced test case deterministically reproduces the exact Firefox-vs-Chrome divergence (iframe 300px vs fills container) and isolates it to auto-width replaced-element sizing, with no console/network factors involved.
result.steps: 1. Read the reproduction script at /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-nlajtpko/2052489-default.repro.mjs to understand the claimed cause (auto-width aspect-ratio iframe sizing). 2. Loaded Firefox and Chrome DevTools MCP tools via ToolSearch and navigated both browsers to https://drmeth.com/. 3. Set both viewports to 1366x768 (Firefox set_viewport_size; Chrome resize_page). 4. Ran evaluate_script in BOTH browsers to measure document.querySelector('iframe'): getBoundingClientRect, getComputedStyle (width/height/aspect-ratio/max-width/max-height/display/flex/align-self/min-width), attribute presence, class name, and the parent flex container's size and computed flex properties. Result: Firefox iframe 300x169 (cssWidth 300px) vs Chrome iframe fills container (cssWidth 1905px); identical classes/CSS, no width/height attribute in either. 5. Checked console errors in both (Firefox list_console_messages level=error; Chrome list_console_messages types=[error]) — none relevant; ruled out JS/network causes. 6. Wrote a minimal reduced test case to /tmp/autowebcompat-diagnosis-nlajtpko/2052489-default.html: three 400x260 flex containers (display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center), each holding a child with aspect-ratio:16/9; max-width:100%; max-height:100% and NO width/height: (A)
